core.intro: Across Australia feijoa is grown much the same way: full sun, free-draining soil, shelter for its brittle wood, a self-fertile variety (or two different ones for a heavier crop), and a little patience for the first few years. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Most named feijoas come from New Zealand breeding. Unique and Apollo are compact, early-bearing and self-fertile, which makes them the easy backyard picks; Mammoth and Triumph are the classic large-fruited flavour varieties; Nazemetz, Duffy, Large Oval and White Goose round out what Australian nurseries usually carry. Despite the common name pineapple guava, feijoa is not a true guava (see our guava page). Decide whether you want one self-fertile tree or a pair of cultivars for cross-pollination, then match a named variety to the space you have. The in-stock table above shows what is available right now.

sections.heading: Pollination: one tree or two?
sections.body: Feijoa flowers are pollinated mainly by birds, which eat the sweet fleshy petals and carry pollen as they go, and to a lesser extent by bees, so a garden that attracts birds sets more fruit. A few cultivars (Unique, Apollo and Coolidge) crop on their own, but every feijoa, even a self-fertile one, fruits more heavily with a second different cultivar nearby, and several older varieties such as Triumph and Superba need a pollinator to crop at all. If you only have room for one tree, choose a reliably self-fertile variety like Unique or Apollo; if you have room for two, pair any two different cultivars and both will bear better.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Feijoa fruits best in full sun (it tolerates part shade) on a slightly acid to neutral, free-draining soil, and it will not tolerate waterlogging, so avoid any spot where water pools. The wood is brittle and breaks in strong wind, so give it a sheltered position or a windbreak; usefully, feijoa itself makes a dense evergreen hedge, screen or windbreak and clips well. Container-grown plants can go in for most of the year as long as you water them through their first summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Feijoa is fairly drought-hardy once established, but it rewards water and the make-or-break time is flowering and the autumn fruit fill: let the tree dry out then and it sheds flowers and small fruit, so water deeply through dry spells from flowering until harvest and keep a good mulch. It is a light feeder, happy with a balanced fertiliser and some compost or well-rotted manure in spring; go easy, since heavy feeding pushes leaves at the expense of fruit.

sections.heading: Harvest
sections.body: Feijoa flowers in late spring and early summer and the fruit ripens through autumn, roughly March to June depending on where you are. The fruit reaches its fullest flavour just as it is ready to drop, and a feijoa picked hard and green never develops that flavour, so either gather fresh windfalls every day (lay straw or net underneath so they stay clean and out of reach of fruit fly) or pick when the fruit gives to a gentle squeeze and parts easily from the branch. A grafted or cutting-grown plant usually starts bearing within two to four years; seedlings take longer.

sections.heading: Eating and using the fruit
sections.body: Cut a ripe feijoa in half and scoop out the soft, aromatic, sweet-tart flesh with a spoon, skin and all left behind. The fruit has a short shelf life and bruises easily, so eat it within a few days, or freeze the scooped pulp and turn a glut into jam, chutney, cordial or a cake. The thick flower petals are edible too: they are sweet and crunchy, good in a salad, and you can pick a few without costing yourself any fruit.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted or cutting-grown cultivar rather than an unnamed seedling. Seedlings are variable, some turn out self-sterile and crop poorly on their own, and they all take longer to bear, while named stock fruits sooner and to type. Check the label for the cultivar name and whether it is self-fertile, and remember that even self-fertile types yield more with a second variety nearby. The in-stock table above lists the named varieties available now and what they cost.

faqs.q: Do feijoas fruit in warm or tropical parts of Australia?
faqs.a: The bush grows, but it crops poorly without a cool winter. Feijoa needs a modest amount of winter chill, on the order of 50 hours, to flower heavily, so in the humid coast and the tropics it tends to give you handsome foliage and few fruit. It is really a cool-climate fruit wearing a subtropical label.

Yes. The fleshy white and crimson petals are sweet and pleasant to eat straight off the bush, and because birds pollinate the flower while nibbling the same petals, you can pick a handful for a salad without reducing your crop. Some people make a delicate jam or syrup from them.

faqs.q: How big does a feijoa get, and can I keep it small or grow it as a hedge?
faqs.a: Left alone it makes a large shrub or small tree of about 3 to 4 metres, but it takes pruning and clipping very well, so it is easily held to 2 metres, grown as a clipped hedge or screen, or kept in a large pot on a courtyard or balcony.

faqs.q: My feijoa flowers every year but sets little fruit. Why?
faqs.a: The usual causes are poor pollination and a too-mild winter. If it is a single self-sterile variety, or few birds and bees are visiting, add a second cultivar and plant to attract birds; if your winters are very mild the tree may not get the chill it needs. Also keep the water up at flowering, since drought then drops both flowers and fruit.

WA.intro: Growing feijoa in Western Australia. Feijoa is one of the most cold-tolerant of all the exotic fruits and a long-standing favourite in Perth gardens, growing happily right across the cooler south-west.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The south-west's Mediterranean climate, with mild wet winters and warm dry summers, suits feijoa well: it gets the light winter chill it needs to flower, and as a tree hardy to around minus 10 degrees it almost never minds the frost. Summer heat above the low to mid 30s can knock back fruit set, so trees in the hot, dry inland and the north need shade and steady water, while the cooler hills and the south coast bring out the best flavour.

sections.heading: Where feijoa grows in WA
sections.body: Feijoa crops across the whole south-west. It does especially well in the cooler Perth Hills of the Darling Range, the Great Southern around Albany, Mount Barker and Denmark, and the South West around Manjimup and Pemberton, and it is a reliable, heavy-bearing tree in suburban Perth and the Swan Valley as well.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA feijoas ripen through autumn, mostly from about April into June. Warm coastal Perth tends to come in a little earlier and the cool Great Southern a little later, and the rule is the same everywhere: take the fruit as it begins to drop, not while it is still hard and green.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Mediterranean fruit fly is established right across WA, from Esperance through to Carnarvon, and it will sting ripening feijoa, so net the tree or run a baiting program and clear fallen fruit promptly. The dry summers keep fungal problems low. Birds are the other competitor for ripe fruit, though they earn their keep by pollinating the flowers.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine means live plants from other states must be permitted on the WA Organism List and are usually certified and treated before entry. Many eastern nurseries therefore will not post plants to WA, so the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here.

faqs.q: Which feijoa varieties suit Perth and the south-west?
faqs.a: Almost any, because the mild winters and warm summers suit the species. For a single self-fertile tree, Unique or Apollo are compact and dependable, and if you have room, adding a second variety such as Mammoth, Duffy or Triumph lifts the crop on both.

faqs.q: Do feijoas need much water on Perth's sandy soils?
faqs.a: Through summer and especially autumn while the fruit is filling, yes. On free-draining Perth sand, dig in compost, mulch thickly and water deeply, because drought stress at fruiting time is the main reason a healthy tree drops its flowers and fruit.

QLD.intro: Growing feijoa in Queensland. Feijoa needs a genuinely cool winter to crop, so in Queensland it belongs in the cool, elevated south rather than the warm coast or the tropics.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Feijoa wants cool winters and dislikes hot, humid, low-chill conditions, so coastal and subtropical Queensland, including Brisbane, is marginal: the bush grows but mild winters trigger little flowering, and summer heat over the mid 30s further cuts fruit set. The wet tropics are unsuitable. On the coast, treat feijoa as an ornamental evergreen and expect light, unreliable crops.

sections.heading: Where feijoa grows in QLD
sections.body: The dependable districts are high and cool. The Granite Belt around Stanthorpe sits near 800 metres with real winter chill and frost, and the southern Darling Downs around Toowoomba is cool enough to crop feijoa well. Production thins out quickly as you drop toward the warm coast.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: In its warmer districts Queensland feijoa ripens a little earlier than the southern states, from about March, while the cool Granite Belt runs later into autumn. Pick as the fruit starts to fall rather than while it is still firm.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic and a major, frequent pest that readily attacks feijoa, so net the tree or run a baiting program and clear fallen fruit. Warm summer humidity also favours fungal problems, which is another reason feijoa is happier in the dry, cool inland than on the coast.

faqs.q: Can you grow feijoa in Brisbane?
faqs.a: You can grow the bush as a hardy evergreen, but reliable fruit is difficult on the warm, humid coast because the winters are too mild to set off heavy flowering. Choose the most reliable self-fertile types, keep your expectations modest, and accept far lighter crops than the cool inland gets.

faqs.q: Will a feijoa fruit better in a pot in coastal Queensland?
faqs.a: A pot will not give it the winter chill it lacks, but it does let you give sharp drainage and stand the tree in the coolest, most open spot, and move it about. Expect lighter crops than the same variety grown in a cool, elevated district like Stanthorpe.

NSW.intro: Growing feijoa in New South Wales. Frost-hardy and cool-loving, feijoa crops well across the NSW tablelands and the cooler coast, and is one of the easier backyard fruit trees in those districts.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Feijoa thrives where winters are cool and summers warm but not scorching. The Central and Southern Tablelands, the Blue Mountains, the Southern Highlands and the cooler Sydney basin all suit it, and an established tree shrugs off the frosts of those districts. The warm, humid far north coast is more marginal, because mild winters give the tree too little chill for a heavy crop.

sections.heading: Where feijoa grows in NSW
sections.body: From the cool Central West around Orange and Bathurst, up through the Blue Mountains and the Southern Highlands, and across the temperate Sydney basin and the south coast, feijoa is a dependable backyard tree. It tails off on the humid northern rivers, where it grows well but fruits less reliably.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: NSW feijoas ripen through autumn, roughly April to June. Warmer coastal districts come in a touch earlier and the cold tablelands a little later, but everywhere the fruit is best taken as it begins to drop rather than picked hard.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is present through the coast and much of the inland and will attack feijoa, so net the tree or bait for it and clear fallen fruit; the cold tablelands have lower fruit-fly pressure, which is part of why feijoa does so well there. Birds are the other competitor for ripe fruit.

faqs.q: Are feijoas frost hardy enough for the NSW tablelands?
faqs.a: Established feijoas are hardy to about minus 10 degrees and take the tablelands' frosts in their stride, which is exactly why they crop so well up there. A very hard frost below about minus 9 degrees can damage the flower buds and trim the next crop, so shelter young plants through their first winter or two.

faqs.q: Do feijoas fruit on the NSW north coast?
faqs.a: The tree grows happily, but it sets less fruit there than on the tablelands because the warm, humid winters do not give it the chill it needs. If you are on the north coast, choose a reliably self-fertile variety and plant two different cultivars to get the best result you can.

VIC.intro: Growing feijoa in Victoria. Victoria's cool winters and mild summers are close to ideal for feijoa, the same conditions that made it a backyard staple across the Tasman in New Zealand.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria gives feijoa just what it wants: a genuinely cool winter for reliable flowering, summers that rarely cook the fruit, and frosts that an established tree handles easily. Cooler districts also draw out the best flavour, so the fruit here is among the best in the country. The main things to manage are sheltering very young plants from hard frost and protecting the brittle wood from wind.

sections.heading: Where feijoa grows in VIC
sections.body: Feijoa crops right across settled Victoria: Melbourne gardens, the Yarra Valley, the Mornington Peninsula, the cooler central districts around Bendigo, and the green south and east through Gippsland. It is one of the most reliable evergreen fruit trees for a Victorian backyard.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria's cooler season ripens feijoa later than the northern states, mainly through May and June. The fruit drops when it is ready, so gather fresh windfalls daily or pick as the fruit begins to loosen, rather than taking it hard and green.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Pest pressure on feijoa is generally low in Victoria, but Queensland fruit fly is now established in parts of the state, including the Goulburn Murray and Sunraysia, so net the tree or bait for it where it occurs and clear fallen fruit. Birds are the other competitor for ripe fruit.

sections.heading: Frost on young trees
sections.body: Established feijoas are very frost-hardy, but a new plant is tender through its first winter or two. In cold central and elevated districts, shelter young trees with frost cloth, give them a warm, north-facing spot, and they will quickly toughen up into one of the hardiest fruit trees in the garden.

faqs.q: Is feijoa a good choice for a cool Melbourne garden?
faqs.a: It is one of the best. Feijoa is fully frost-hardy once established, fruits reliably on Victoria's cool winters, and doubles as an evergreen hedge with silver-backed leaves and edible flowers, so it earns its place in the garden well before the autumn crop arrives.

faqs.q: Feijoa is sold as a subtropical fruit, so why does it do so well in cold Victoria?
faqs.a: Because the subtropical label is misleading. Feijoa actually needs a cool winter to flower heavily and develops better flavour in cooler areas, so Victoria's climate suits it better than the warm, humid north of the country does.
